lens: PERFORMANCE-FIRST: maximize frame rate at massive object counts and across ~5 orders of magnitude of scale, via instanced rendering, zero-allocation hot loops, SoA typed-array data, and uniform world-rescaling for float precision.
summary: A Katamari-style roller built on Three.js r177 + Vite, pure static deploy. The core trick for both seamlessness and float precision is the same mechanism: all simulation runs in "sim units" kept in a narrow numeric band (ball radius always between 0.5 and 3.0 sim units), and when the ball crosses a tier threshold we apply an instantaneous uniform similarity transform (multiply every position/radius/velocity by S=0.2 in one frame). Because a uniform scale about the origin, with a camera whose pose is derived from scaled quantities, is a similarity transform, the rendered image before and after rescale is pixel-identical — zero hitch, zero pop. True size for the HUD is tracked separately as simRadius × worldScale (a plain double, displayed as cm/m/km). Physics is custom arcade physics (~6 KB) instead of rapier/cannon: the game needs only a sphere rolling on a plane plus sphere-vs-static-object overlap, which a uniform-grid spatial hash over SoA typed arrays does in microseconds; a 600 KB+ WASM rigid-body solver buys nothing here. Rendering is one InstancedMesh per archetype (8 archetypes × 3 live tiers ≈ 24 world draw calls) with merged-primitive vertex-colored geometry so every object shares one material. Stuck objects become instances in per-archetype InstancedMeshes parented to the ball — their instance matrices are written once in ball-local space at absorb time and never touched again (zero per-frame cost), with a ring-buffer cap and shrink-out of sub-pixel items. Spawning is deterministic, chunk-based, and amortized (≤64 instance writes/frame). Total budget: ≤60 draw calls, ≤600k triangles, 0 bytes/frame steady-state GC garbage, 60 fps on integrated GPUs.
scaleSystem:
TIER TABLE (true radius thresholds, ×5 per tier; ball starts at 5 cm):
- T0 "Desk":   5 cm → 25 cm   (thumbtacks, coins, dice, erasers, paperclips, candies, batteries, keys)
- T1 "Room":   25 cm → 1.25 m (books, bottles, shoes, cats, potted plants, lamps, stools, toys)
- T2 "Street": 1.25 m → 6 m   (bicycles, people, benches, mailboxes, signs, dogs, carts, hydrants)
- T3 "Town":   6 m → 30 m     (cars, trucks, trees, kiosks, small houses, buses, fountains, sheds)
- T4 "City":   30 m → 150 m   (buildings, cranes, bridges segments, big trees, water towers, billboards)
- T5 "Skyline":150 m → 750 m  (skyscrapers, stadiums, hills, ferris wheels) — win screen at 500 m.

SIM-UNIT INVARIANT: ball radius lives in [0.5, 3.0] sim units. Tier-up trigger: simRadius ≥ 2.5 → apply rescale factor S = 0.2 (so simRadius becomes 0.5). worldScale (a JS double in ScaleManager) is divided by S; trueRadius = simRadius × worldScale, used only for HUD and tier-catalog selection. All world coordinates therefore stay within ±2048 sim units forever → full Float32 mantissa precision for render matrices, no jitter, no growing numbers.

RESCALE ALGORITHM (runs inside one frame, between physics update and render — the frame rendered after rescale is mathematically identical to the frame that would have rendered without it):
```
function tierUp():
  S = 0.2
  worldScale /= S                       // double accumulator for true size
  ball.pos *= S; ball.radius *= S; ball.vel *= S   // angularVel unchanged (rad/s is scale-free)
  for i in objectStore.alive:           // ~4000 items, SoA Float32Array, < 0.3 ms
    px[i]*=S; py[i]*=S; pz[i]*=S; radius[i]*=S
  ballGroup.scale *= S                  // stuck instances ride along for free (ball-local space)
  spatialHash.rebuild(cellSize = TIER[t+1].cellSizeSim)   // ~4000 inserts into flat arrays, < 0.5 ms
  for each InstancedMesh: rewriteMatricesFromStore()      // batch write, mark needsUpdate once
  fog.density /= S; dirLight.position *= S; blobShadow.scale *= S
  tierIndex++; emit('tierChanged', {tierIndex, trueRadius})
```
Camera never needs special handling: CameraRig computes position every frame as ballPos + dir × (k × simRadius) with k≈7, height ≈ 3.5 × simRadius — a pure function of scaled state, so the similarity transform preserves the image exactly. Camera distance is CONTINUOUS in radius (the "pull back" is just growth, not a tier event), so there is no camera pop at tier boundaries either.

POPULATION CROSS-FADE (the visible part of "one step larger", decoupled from the math rescale): three tiers are live at all times — tier N-1 (absorbable leftovers, fading), tier N (current targets), tier N+1 (scenery, too big to absorb, becomes absorbable next tier). When trueRadius reaches 70% of the N→N+1 threshold, the Spawner starts populating tier N+2 chunks as scenery (amortized ≤64 spawns/frame, so they appear over ~2 s at the horizon where pop-in is invisible behind fog). When the threshold is crossed: tier N-1 objects begin a 1.5 s scale-to-zero fade-out (driven by a per-instance scale factor in the instance matrix; they are by then < 2% of screen-space ball size, i.e. sub-pixel, so the fade is imperceptible) and their hash entries are removed immediately so they cost zero physics. Hysteresis: tier-down (shrinking) is impossible in this game, so no oscillation handling is needed; the 0.5→2.5 sim band itself is the hysteresis.

FLOATING ORIGIN (secondary guard): if |ball.pos| > 1500 sim units, subtract ball.pos from every position and the ball in the same between-update-and-render slot (also a perfect-identity transform for the camera-relative image). With ×5 rescales this rarely triggers, but it makes infinite roaming safe.
physics:
DECISION: custom arcade physics. Justification for THIS game: (1) Required features are exactly: a kinematic sphere rolling on a flat ground plane, sphere-vs-static-sphere/AABB overlap tests, and a push-back response against non-absorbable statics. No stacking, no joints, no dynamic-vs-dynamic, no convex hulls. (2) rapier3d is ~1.7 MB WASM (~600 KB gzip) plus async init; cannon-es is ~150 KB but its JS narrowphase/solver costs milliseconds at 4000 bodies and fights GC. Custom physics is ~6 KB and runs the entire step in < 0.2 ms. (3) A physics engine would still need us to manage body activation per tier and would break under world rescaling (engines hate teleport-scaling all bodies every tier). (4) Determinism: custom + seeded PRNG gives reproducible runs for free.

BALL MODEL: fixed timestep 60 Hz with accumulator (cap 3 substeps, no interpolation — at 60 Hz it is unnecessary). State: pos(Vector3), vel(Vector3), quat(Quaternion), radius. Input applies acceleration in camera-relative ground plane (accel = 22 × radius sim-units/s², speedCap = 9 × radius/s — scaling with radius keeps feel identical across tiers and across rescales). Friction: vel *= pow(0.92, dt×60). Rolling without slipping: angularAxis = up × vel.normalized, angularSpeed = |vel|/radius; integrate quat by axis-angle dq each step (scratch quaternion, no allocation). Ground: pos.y = radius (flat plane; optional gentle heightfield later).

SPATIAL HASH (objects are static after spawn — insert once, remove on absorb/despawn):
Uniform grid, cellSize = 2 × median archetype radius of current tier (per-tier config, e.g. T0: 0.8 sim units). Storage is flat typed arrays (zero object allocation): `cellStart: Int32Array(numCells+1)`, `cellEntries: Int32Array(maxObjects)` rebuilt by counting sort on rebuild; incremental removes use a tombstone flag checked at query time, with full rebuild on tier-up (when it is cheap and mandatory anyway). Hash key = ((xi×73856093) ^ (zi×19349663)) & (TABLE_SIZE-1), TABLE_SIZE = 16384 (2D hash — y is irrelevant on a plane).
```
function queryBall(ballPos, ballR, maxObjR, outIndices):
  reach = ballR + maxObjR                      // maxObjR from current tier config
  for xi in floor((x-reach)/cs) .. floor((x+reach)/cs):
   for zi in floor((z-reach)/cs) .. floor((z+reach)/cs):
     h = hash(xi, zi)
     for k in cellStart[h] .. cellStart[h+1]:
       i = cellEntries[k]
       if flags[i] & ALIVE and not tombstoned:
         dx=px[i]-x; dz=pz[i]-z; dy=py[i]-y
         if dx*dx+dy*dy+dz*dz < (ballR+radius[i])^2: outIndices.push(i)  // preallocated Int32 scratch
```
Typical query touches 9–25 cells, ~5–40 candidates: microseconds.

ABSORB / COLLIDE RULE per overlapping object i:
- absorbable if radius[i] ≤ ball.radius × 0.62 → emit absorb: growth newR = cbrt(R³ + 0.35 × r_i³) (volume-based, k=0.35 tuned so each tier takes ~60–90 s); object removed from store/hash; Ball module attaches stuck instance; HUD event fired.
- else (too big): treat as static sphere/AABB — push ball out along contact normal (position correction), reflect normal component of vel × 0.3 (soft bounce), tangential preserved. Optional "knock off" juice (drop 1–2 recent stuck items) is a stretch goal, off by default.

GC PRESSURE: physics owns zero per-frame allocations. All candidate lists, scratch vectors/quaternions, and event payload objects are module-level reused instances. Object data is SoA: px/py/pz/radius Float32Array, archetypeId Uint16Array, tierOf Uint8Array, flags Uint8Array, instanceSlot Int32Array — capacity 8192, free-list allocator (core/pool.js).
rendering:
DRAW-CALL BUDGET: ≤ 60 total, typical ~38.
- World objects: one InstancedMesh per (archetype, tier) — 8 archetypes × 3 live tiers = 24 draws. Capacity per InstancedMesh = tier spawn budget for that archetype (128–512). count is set to the high-water mark; dead instances are compacted by swap-with-last (O(1), updates instanceSlot of the moved object).
- Stuck objects on ball: 8 InstancedMeshes (one per archetype family, capacity 512 total across all, ring buffer) parented to the ball Object3D = 8 draws. KEY OPTIMIZATION: instance matrices are in BALL-LOCAL space, written exactly once at absorb time; rolling/translation is inherited from the parent — per-frame cost of 500 stuck objects is ONE matrix multiply (the parent's), not 500.
- Ball core: 1 draw — icosphere (3 subdivisions) with per-vertex displacement noise whose amplitude grows with absorb count (cheap "mass" illusion under the stuck layer).
- Environment: ground plane (1, shader gradient + procedural canvas grid texture generated at boot), sky dome (1, vertex-gradient), blob shadow decal under ball (1, canvas radial-gradient texture). Lights: 1 hemisphere + 1 directional, NO shadow maps (blob shadow only — shadow maps double draw calls and murder integrated GPUs).

MATERIALS: exactly 3 materials in the whole game — MeshLambertMaterial({vertexColors:true}) for all objects and the ball, ShaderMaterial for ground/sky, MeshBasicMaterial(transparent) for the blob shadow. Geometry per archetype is a merged composite of 2–6 primitives (Box/Cylinder/Sphere/Cone/Torus, low segment counts: spheres 10×8, cylinders 10) via BufferGeometryUtils.mergeGeometries, with colors baked into a `color` vertex attribute at build time (geometryFactory). Triangle budget per archetype ≤ 350 tris → worst case 24 × 512 × 350 ≈ but real instance counts keep the scene ≤ 600k tris; verified via renderer.info in the debug overlay.

STUCK-OBJECT COST CONTROL: (1) ring buffer cap 512 — overwriting the oldest slot is correct because the oldest stuck items are exactly the ones that are now sub-pixel; (2) continuous cull: any stuck instance whose attachRadius < 2% of current ball radius gets its matrix zero-scaled (invisible, slot reclaimable) — at ×5 tier growth this clears a whole tier's stickers within two tiers; (3) the displaced ball core visually replaces the lost detail. This avoids runtime geometry merging/baking entirely (merge-baking causes multi-ms BufferGeometry rebuild spikes; the ring buffer achieves the same render cost with zero spikes). 

CROSS-FADE WITHOUT TRANSPARENCY: tier fade-in/out is done by animating per-instance scale (matrix), never material opacity — keeps everything opaque, one shader program, no sorting, no overdraw blow-up. Distance fog (scene.fog, density derived from camera distance) hides spawn-in at the horizon.

CULLING: frustum culling must be set frustumCulled=false on InstancedMeshes (Three culls by whole-mesh bounding sphere; we keep one InstancedMesh per archetype spanning the active area, and instead cull by SPAWN RADIUS — objects only exist within ~140 sim units of the ball, which the fog matches). This trades a few off-screen instances for zero per-frame culling CPU and stable draw calls.

UPLOAD AMORTIZATION: instanceMatrix.needsUpdate is set at most once per mesh per frame; spawn/despawn writes are batched and capped at 64 instance writes/frame; usage = DynamicDrawUsage on instanceMatrix.

RENDERER SETTINGS: antialias true, pixelRatio min(devicePixelRatio, 1.75), powerPreference 'high-performance'. Debug overlay (toggle with backquote) shows fps, draw calls (renderer.info.render.calls), triangles, alive objects, GC-visible heap delta.
spawning:
OBJECT CATALOG: 8 archetypes per tier × 6 tiers = 48 entries in src/config/catalog.js. Each archetype: id, tier, buildGeometry(rng) (returns merged vertex-colored BufferGeometry, built ONCE at boot for all 48 — total boot cost < 80 ms), nominal radiusSim (relative to its tier's unit), radiusJitter (±25%), spawnWeight, yOffset, randomYaw flag. Examples: T0 {thumbtack: cone+cylinder, coin: squat cylinder, die: box}, T3 {car: 2 boxes + 4 cylinder wheels, tree: cone×2 + cylinder trunk}, T4 {building: box stack with window-grid vertex coloring}.

PLACEMENT — deterministic chunk grid: world is divided per-tier into chunks of 32 sim units. For a chunk (cx, cz, tier), a mulberry32 PRNG seeded with hash(worldSeed, cx, cz, tier) generates N = tier.objectsPerChunk placements (jittered sub-grid: chunk split into N cells, one object per cell at cell center + jitter×0.8 — Poisson-quality spacing at zero cost, no overlap tests needed). Same chunk always generates the same objects → despawned-then-revisited areas regenerate identically (absorbed objects are remembered in a per-chunk consumed bitmask, Map<chunkKey, Uint32Array> — the only Map in the hot path, touched only on chunk load/unload).

BUDGETS (alive simultaneously): current tier N ≈ 2000 objects (load radius 96 sim units, ~28 chunks × 72/chunk), tier N+1 scenery ≈ 600 (load radius 140, sparser, 8/chunk), tier N-1 leftovers ≤ 1200 and falling (no new spawns, fade out on tier-up). Total alive ≤ 4096 (objectStore capacity 8192 for headroom). Per frame the Spawner: (1) computes ball's chunk coords per live tier; (2) diffs against loaded-chunk set (Set of int keys, ~30 entries — churns only when crossing a chunk boundary); (3) enqueues spawn/despawn work into a ring queue; (4) drains ≤ 64 spawns + 64 despawns per frame (instance matrix write + hash insert each ≈ 1 µs). Chunk loading is biased to spawn front-of-motion chunks first.

DESPAWN: leaving load radius → remove from hash, swap-compact instance, free SoA slot, record nothing (consumed bitmask only tracks absorbed). Tier-up → tier N-2 chunks all unloaded over 1.5 s fade; consumed bitmasks for dead tiers are deleted (bounded memory).

SEEDING: worldSeed from URL ?seed= or Date.now(); shown on game-over screen for shareable runs.

fileLayout.responsibility: Static shell: canvas mount, HUD/title DOM skeleton, loads /src/main.js as module.
Vite config: base './', build target es2022, single-chunk output for Cloudflare Pages.
Bootstrap + fixed-timestep 60 Hz game loop + game state machine (title/playing/win) wiring all modules.
JSDoc typedefs for Tier, Archetype, events, input state (no runtime code).
Tiny pub/sub emitter with reused payload objects (zero-allocation emit).
mulberry32 seeded PRNG + integer hash for (seed, cx, cz, tier) chunk seeding.
Free-list index allocator and ring-buffer helpers over typed arrays.
TIERS array (6 tiers: thresholds, cell sizes, load radii, budgets) + RESCALE_S constant.
48 archetype definitions (8 per tier): geometry builders, radii, weights, palettes.
ObjectStore: SoA typed arrays (pos/radius/archetype/flags/instanceSlot), alloc/free.
Uniform-grid 2D spatial hash on flat Int32Arrays: rebuild, insert, tombstone-remove, queryBall.
Chunk-based deterministic placement, amortized spawn/despawn queues, consumed bitmasks.
worldScale double, tier-up detection, one-frame similarity rescale, true-size formatting.
Ball kinematics: accel/friction/speed cap, rolling-without-slipping quaternion integration, ground clamp.
Hash query each step, absorb-vs-pushback decision (0.62 rule), volume growth, collision response.
WebGLRenderer setup, resize, pixelRatio cap, render call, renderer.info debug stats overlay.
Builds all 48 merged, vertex-colored composite primitive geometries at boot.
InstancedMesh manager per (archetype,tier): slot alloc, swap-compact, scale-fade animation, batched needsUpdate.
Katamari group: displaced icosphere core, stuck-instance ring buffer (512), attachStuck, 2% sub-pixel cull.
Follow camera: distance/height pure function of simRadius, yaw from input, exponential smoothing.
Ground shader plane, gradient sky dome, fog, hemisphere+directional lights, blob shadow decal.
Keyboard (WASD/arrows) + optional mouse-steer + touch joystick, normalized {forward, turn} state.
DOM HUD: cm/m/km size readout, absorb counter, tier banner, progress bar; 10 Hz diffed updates.
Title screen, win screen, restart overlay; emits game:start / game:reset.

risks: Rescale-frame spike: rewriting ~4000 instance matrices + hash rebuild in one frame. Mitigation: SoA batch loops measured target < 1.5 ms total; if exceeded on low-end, pre-stage next-tier matrices the frame before and only flip needsUpdate on the rescale frame.
Spawn pop-in visible if fog density vs loadRadiusSim is mistuned per tier — must enforce invariant fogVisibility < loadRadius - chunkSize in tiers.js and assert it in dev mode.

Stuck-object 512 ring cap could look sparse if a player absorbs hundreds within one tier; mitigation is the displacement-noise ball core growing with count — needs art tuning, flagged as the main 'feel' risk.

frustumCulled=false trades GPU vertex work for CPU savings; on very weak iGPUs vertex cost of off-screen instances may matter — fallback: split each archetype InstancedMesh into 4 quadrant meshes (raises draw calls to ~80 but restores coarse culling).

Per-instance scale-fade requires decomposing/recomposing matrices for fading instances each frame (≤1200 during a transition window); budgeted at 0.3 ms but must be amortized (e.g. update fades in 4 interleaved cohorts) if profiling shows spikes.
Tombstoned hash entries degrade query constant between rebuilds in long single-tier sessions; mitigation: opportunistic rebuild when tombstone ratio > 25% (still < 0.5 ms).
Three.js r177 API drift (e.g. BufferGeometryUtils import path, InstancedMesh DynamicDrawUsage) — pin exact version in package.json and verify the three/addons import map at project start.
Touch input is declared a bonus; the camera-relative steering model must not bake in pointer-lock assumptions or the touch path becomes a rewrite.

lens: GAME-FEEL-FIRST: every architectural decision below is justified first by how it serves rolling momentum, stick-to-ball satisfaction, camera behavior, and the joy of the scale-transition moment; performance techniques are chosen so they never produce a visible hitch that would break that feel.
summary: A static Three.js r177 + Vite site with a custom ~400-line arcade physics core (no physics library) built around one central insight: if camera distance, fog, movement speed, and spawn density are all defined as multiples of the ball radius, then the game is scale-invariant — the "seamless tier transition" stops being an event to engineer and becomes the absence of any event. There is no absorbability gate at tier boundaries: any object whose bounding diameter is at most 0.9 × ball radius is absorbable at any moment, so "previously-large objects become absorbable" happens continuously, object by object, never as a pop. Tiers exist only as content bands (what spawns, fog color, HUD label) plus a celebration moment (FOV kick, HUD banner, odometer unit rollover). Float precision is handled by an invisible one-frame world rescale (×0.2 around the ball) whenever sim radius exceeds 20 units; because every visual quantity is radius-proportional, the rendered frame before and after rescale is pixel-identical. Rendering is one InstancedMesh per archetype (6 archetypes × 4 active tiers ≈ 24 world draw calls), stuck objects live in per-archetype instanced pools parented to the ball and are progressively baked into 1–2 merged "crust" meshes, keeping total draw calls under 70. Objects are placed by deterministic seeded-hash chunk spawning with per-frame budgets (≤24 spawns, ≤32 fade writes) so no frame ever does a burst of work. The codebase is ~26 small ES modules with JSDoc types, communicating through a tiny EventBus and three shared data shapes (TierConfig, Archetype, WorldObject/StuckRecord), so HUD, rendering, physics, spawning, and feel-tuning can be developed in parallel.
scaleSystem:
UNITS. Two coordinate systems: SIM units (what physics/rendering use, ball radius always kept in [4, 20]) and REAL meters for display. `worldScale` = meters per sim unit. realRadius = simRadius × worldScale. Shape: `ScaleState = { simRadius:number, worldScale:number, tierIndex:number, transitionT:number /*0..1 during tier celebration*/ }` owned by src/game/scaleManager.js.

TIERS (content bands, 5× per step, indexed by realRadius of the BALL; object size band given as bounding-sphere diameter in real meters):
- T0 "Desk"   ball 0.04–0.20 m; objects 0.01–0.10 m (thumbtack, coin, die, eraser, candy, pencil stub)
- T1 "Room"   ball 0.20–1.0 m;  objects 0.08–0.50 m (book, mug, potted plant, cat, sneaker, lamp)
- T2 "Street" ball 1.0–5.0 m;   objects 0.4–2.5 m  (traffic cone, bike, person, bench, mailbox, dog)
- T3 "Town"   ball 5–25 m;      objects 2–12 m     (car, truck, tree, kiosk, bus, shed)
- T4 "City"   ball 25–125 m;    objects 10–60 m    (house, mid-rise, crane, billboard, water tower, overpass chunk)
- T5 "Metro"  ball 125–625 m;   objects 50–300 m   (skyscraper, stadium, ferris wheel, hill, bridge span, gas tank)
tierIndex = floor(log5(realRadius / 0.04)), clamped 0..5. Tuned so each tier takes ~45–70 absorbs (~60–90 s).

WHY IT'S SEAMLESS (the core trick): the camera, fog, speed and absorb rule never reference tierIndex.
- Absorbable: objDiameter ≤ ballRadius × 0.9 — evaluated per object per overlap, continuously. A car becomes absorbable the exact frame you're big enough, regardless of tier. Nothing "unlocks".
- Camera distance target = simRadius × 6.5; height = simRadius × 3.2; fog near/far = simRadius × (14 / 55). All follow radius continuously through a damped spring (see rendering), so "camera pulls back" is just normal growth, with the spring lag making big absorbs feel like a satisfying zoom-out breath.
- Tiny-object despawn is per-object, not per-tier: when objDiameter < ballRadius × 0.05 (sub-3-pixels on screen since screen size ∝ size/cameraDist ∝ size/radius), the object fades over 0.6 s by lerping its instance matrix scale to 0, then frees its slot. A round-robin sweep checks 200 objects/frame so the cost is constant.
- Next-tier objects are ALREADY in the world as scenery/obstacles long before they're absorbable (spawner keeps tiers T-1..T+2 populated), so nothing spawns at the threshold moment.

WHAT tierIndex DOES drive: (a) spawner content ranges (which archetype bands chunk-spawn around you), (b) fog/sky palette crossfade over 2 s, (c) HUD tier name + unit formatting, (d) the CELEBRATION: on tierUp event — FOV eases 60→68→60 over 0.8 s, camera spring stiffness temporarily loosened for an extra pull-back overshoot, a one-shot sparkle ring particle burst, rising 3-note WebAudio arpeggio, HUD banner ("You feel bigger! — STREET") and the size odometer rolls its unit (cm→m→km) with a digit-flip animation. This is pure garnish; gameplay state does not branch on it, which is what guarantees no hitch.

FLOAT PRECISION — one-frame world rescale: when simRadius > 20: factor k = 0.2. In a single frame: (1) ballPos → origin translation T = -ballPos; (2) for every live WorldObject: pos = (pos + T) × k, size ×= k; (3) ball: simRadius ×= k (→ 4), velocity ×= k, position = origin; worldScale ÷= k; (4) rebuild both spatial hashes by re-insertion (~2–3k objects, <1 ms); (5) rewrite all instance matrices (~3k writes, ~1.5 ms, measured budget); (6) camera position/fog recomputed from radius as always; (7) crust/stuck meshes are children of the ball group and ball-local, so untouched. Because cameraDist, fog, ground-shader pattern scale, and speeds are all ∝ radius, frame N and N+1 render identically — verified in dev by a debug key that forces rescale and diffs a screenshot. Pseudocode:
```
function maybeRescale():
  if simRadius <= RESCALE_TRIGGER (20): return
  k = 0.2; T = -ball.pos
  for obj in liveObjects: obj.pos = (obj.pos + T) * k; obj.size *= k
  ball.pos = 0; ball.vel *= k; simRadius *= k; worldScale /= k
  spawner.shiftChunkOrigin(T, k)        // re-key active chunks deterministically
  hashSmall.rebuild(liveObjects); hashLarge.rebuild(liveObjects)
  instances.rewriteAll()                 // budgeted single-frame pass
  emit('rescale', {k})                   // camera/fog/audio re-derive from radius (no-op visually)
```
Positions stay within ±~2500 sim units (active ring is radius×~80 max), so float32 instance matrices never see precision shimmer.
physics:
DECISION: custom arcade physics. Rejected rapier3d (~1.3–2 MB wasm+js — heavy for a static-site perf showcase; solves rigid-body dynamics we don't have: world objects are static until absorbed, the ball is a driven kinematic sphere; wasm boundary makes per-object hooks for absorb/feel awkward) and cannon-es (~150 KB but slow beyond ~1k bodies and its contact solver fights arcade tuning — Katamari feel is deliberately non-physical). Custom is ~400 LOC, 0 KB dependency, fully deterministic, and every constant is a feel knob.

BALL MODEL (src/game/katamari.js), fixed timestep 1/60 inside an accumulator loop:
- Input intent {x,y} is camera-relative. accel = 28 × simRadius (units/s²); maxSpeed = 8.5 × simRadius (units/s). Radius-proportional speeds keep screen-space feel identical at every scale; the sensation of "getting huge" is delivered by camera lag, FOV kicks and content, not by sluggishness.
- Velocity damping 0.92^(dt×60) when no input (long satisfying coast); turning blends velocity direction toward input with turnRate 4.2/s — momentum-preserving, so whipping a U-turn carries a drift.
- Rolling without slipping: each step, axis = normalize(up × v), angle = |v|·dt / simRadius, ballQuat = quatFromAxisAngle(axis, angle) × ballQuat. This exact constraint is non-negotiable for feel — stuck objects sweeping over the top and under is the core fantasy.
- Ground: y = 0 plane; ball center y springs to simRadius (small overshoot on growth = a tiny satisfying "pop up" each absorb).
- Mass feel: each absorb multiplies a transient sluggishness factor (accel × 0.97, recovering over 1.5 s) so chaining absorbs feels weighty without ruining top speed.

COLLISION (src/game/absorption.js + src/game/spatialHash.js): objects are bounding spheres (composite primitives baked, sphere fits fine for arcade). Two uniform-grid spatial hashes to handle 100×-mixed sizes: hashSmall (cellSize = simRadius-band-tuned, rebuilt-target ~2.5× median small object size, default 6 sim units) for tiers ≤ current, hashLarge (cellSize 40) for tiers above. Hash is `Map<int key, int[] objectIds>` with key = (cx*73856093) ^ (cz*19349663) on floor(x/cell), floor(z/cell); objects re-bucket only on rescale (they're static).
```
function queryNearby(hash, pos, r):
  minCx=floor((pos.x-r)/cell) … maxCz=floor((pos.z+r)/cell)
  for cx,cz in that range: out.pushAll(hash.get(key(cx,cz)) ?? [])
  return out   // typically 3–9 cells, 0–40 ids
function resolveBall():
  for id in queryNearby(hashSmall, ball.pos, simRadius*1.2) + queryNearby(hashLarge, …):
    obj = objects[id]; if obj.state != 'alive': continue
    d = dist(ball.pos, obj.pos); overlap = simRadius + obj.size*0.5*0.6 - d   // 0.6 = generous absorb core
    if overlap <= 0: continue
    if obj.size <= simRadius * 0.9: absorb(obj)            // see spawning/stuck sections
    else:                                                   // too big: bounce
      n = normalize(ball.pos - obj.pos, planar)
      ball.pos += n * overlap; ball.vel = reflect(ball.vel, n) * 0.35
      if impactSpeed > 5*simRadius: knockOff(min(3, recentStuck)) ; emit('bounce',{speed})
```
Bounce emits camera micro-shake (0.15 × simRadius amplitude, 6 Hz, 0.25 s decay) + synthesized "clonk"; knocked-off stuck objects re-enter the world with a pop velocity and are re-absorbable — a signature Katamari feel beat that custom physics makes trivial and a physics engine would make painful. Total physics cost at 6k objects: one ball, ~12 cell lookups, <0.3 ms/frame.
rendering:
BUDGETS: ≤ 70 draw calls, ≤ 350k triangles, ≤ 6,500 live instances, pixelRatio capped at 1.5 with dynamic resolution fallback (drop to 1.0 if 3-second rolling average frame > 17 ms). No shadow maps — single directional + hemisphere light, MeshLambertMaterial with per-instance color; the ball gets a procedural blob shadow (one circular-gradient canvas-generated alpha quad scaled to radius) which reads better at every scale than a shadow map ever would and costs 1 draw call.

WORLD OBJECTS — one InstancedMesh per archetype: 6 archetypes × 4 active tiers (T-1 fading, T, T+1 obstacles, T+2 sparse scenery) = 24 InstancedMeshes, capacity 256 each (T+2 capped at 64). Each archetype geometry is a pre-merged composite of primitives (BoxGeometry/Cylinder/Cone/Sphere/Torus, ≤ 220 verts) with baked vertex colors for two-tone detail; per-instance tint via instanceColor. Slot allocation by free-list (src/render/instances.js InstancedPool); dead/faded instances get a zero-scale matrix and count stays at high-water mark (cheaper than compaction; zero-scale tris are rejected before raster). frustumCulled = false per mesh (instances span the view anyway); culling is gameplay-driven: the spawner's despawn ring (distance > simRadius × 80 behind the ball) frees slots, and fog (near radius×14, far radius×55) hides both the spawn-in edge and the despawn edge — fog is the feel-preserving culling tool.

STUCK OBJECTS — three-stage lifetime, designed so the freshest absorbs are the most visually alive:
1. ATTACH ANIMATION (the money shot): on absorb the object leaves its world pool slot and for 0.15 s lerps position from world pos to its ball-surface socket with an ease-out and a 1.15→1.0 squash, while the ball's y-spring pops. Then it enters a stuck pool.
2. STUCK POOLS: per-archetype InstancedMeshes parented to the ball's Object3D (so ball quaternion rotates them for free — zero per-frame matrix updates for stuck objects; we only write a matrix once at attach). Socket math (ball-local, survives all growth/rescale):
```
function attach(obj):
  local = inverse(ball.quat).rotate(obj.pos - ball.pos)
  rec = { archetypeId, dirLocal: normalize(local), depthFactor: 0.92,   // 8% embedded
          scaleAtAttach: obj.size / simRadiusAtAttach,                   // RELATIVE size
          quatLocal: randomYawAroundNormal(dirLocal), color: obj.color }
  // per-frame implicit transform (baked into the instance matrix once, in ball-local space):
  //   pos = dirLocal * simRadius * depthFactor   — but since pool is ball-child and we store
  //   matrices in ball-local units, we instead set matrix once at attach using current simRadius
  //   and rely on (3) below to retire it before relative size error matters.
```
Stuck instances do NOT grow with the ball — their absolute size is frozen — which is exactly correct: as you grow they become relatively smaller, naturally sinking toward "texture". Live stuck cap: 600 instances across ≤ 12 archetype pools (only the most recent ~2 tiers of archetypes keep live pools; ~12 extra draw calls).
3. CRUST BAKING: when live stuck count > 600 OR a stuck object's scaleAtAttach × (radiusAtAttach/simRadius) < 0.04, the oldest/smallest ~150 records are baked into a merged BufferGeometry ("crust", ball-local, vertex colors, BufferGeometryUtils.mergeGeometries) attached as one ball-child mesh. Bake is amortized over 3 frames: frame 1 collect transforms, frame 2 merge into a pre-allocated buffer (~150 × 220 verts ≈ 33k verts, ~3–4 ms worst case, acceptable as it never co-occurs with rescale), frame 3 swap mesh + free 150 instance slots. Max 2 crust meshes; when an entire crust's contents fall below 1% of radius it is dropped and replaced by bumping the ball core's vertex-noise displacement uniform — the ball permanently "remembers" its history as lumpiness. Crust = 1–2 draw calls regardless of thousands absorbed.

BALL CORE: icosphere (3 subdivisions, ~640 tris) with a vertex shader doing seeded noise displacement whose amplitude grows with total absorbs, and a base color that lerps 10% toward each absorbed object's color — the ball becomes a swirl of what you've eaten.

CAMERA (src/render/cameraRig.js — feel centerpiece): position target = ballPos - moveDir × (simRadius × 6.5) + up × (simRadius × 3.2); look target = ballPos + vel × 0.4 (look-ahead makes speed feel faster). Both run through critically-damped springs (stiffness 6.0 position, 4.5 look) — the spring lag IS the growth feedback: absorb something big and the world visibly recedes over half a second. Yaw follows movement direction lazily (no manual camera needed; mouse drag optionally adds yaw offset that decays). FOV base 60; tierUp kicks to 68 and eases back over 0.8 s; speed > 80% of max adds +4 FOV for a rush effect. Micro-shake on bounce as described in physics.

DRAW-CALL LEDGER: 24 world pools + 12 stuck pools + 2 crusts + 1 ball + 1 blob shadow + 1 ground (single plane, shader with radius-scaled procedural grid/checker so it reads at every tier) + 1 sky gradient dome + ~3 effect meshes (sparkle ring, absorb-pop instanced quads, speed lines) ≈ 45–50 typical, 70 hard cap.
spawning:
CATALOG (src/config/catalog.js): 36 archetypes (6 per tier × 6 tiers), each entirely code-generated. Shape:
```js
/** @typedef {Object} Archetype
 *  @property {string} id            // 'mug'
 *  @property {number} tier          // 1
 *  @property {[number,number]} sizeRange  // real-m bounding diameter, e.g. [0.09, 0.14]
 *  @property {number} weight        // spawn weight within tier
 *  @property {number[]} palette     // 4-6 hex tints for instanceColor
 *  @property {(rng:()=>number)=>THREE.BufferGeometry} build  // merged primitives + vertex colors, ≤220 verts
 *  @property {boolean} upright      // random yaw only vs full random tilt
 */
```
Example recipes: mug = cylinder + half-torus handle; person = capsule + sphere head + box base (vertex-colored shirt/pants); car = box body + box cabin + 4 cylinder wheels; skyscraper = stacked boxes with vertex-color window bands. geometryFactory builds each archetype ONCE at boot (36 merges, ~30 ms total during title screen).

PLACEMENT — deterministic chunked hashing (no stored world): each tier has its own chunk grid, chunkSize = tierObjectMax × 12 (in sim units, recomputed after rescale via worldScale). A chunk's contents are a pure function of (tierId, chunkX, chunkZ, runSeed) via mulberry32: object count uniform in [4, 9] per chunk (T+2 scenery: [1, 3]), each object gets archetype (weighted), size in sizeRange, position jittered in chunk, yaw, palette index. Determinism means despawned-then-revisited chunks regenerate identically (minus a per-run absorbed-ID Set, kept per chunk key, capped — distant chunk records older than 2 tiers are forgotten, invisible at that relative scale).

ACTIVE RING + BUDGETS: for tiers T-1..T+2, chunks within radius simRadius × 80 of the ball are "wanted". Per frame: diff wanted vs active chunk sets (cheap, ~10–40 chunks per tier), enqueue spawns/despawns; execute ≤ 24 spawns and ≤ 32 despawn/fade matrix writes per frame from the queues. Spawn-in has no pop: objects materialize beyond the fog far plane (radius × 55 < ring radius × 80) — fog does the work; as a belt-and-suspenders, spawns inside fog range (only after rescale ring re-keying) scale 0→1 over 0.4 s. Despawn: behind-ball ring exit → fade-out 0.4 s → free slot. Steady-state live counts: T-1 ~150 (shrinking), T ~250, T+1 ~180, T+2 ~40 ≈ 600–900 visible, ≤ 6.5k including not-yet-faded and pools' high-water slack.

TIER HANDOFF: when tierIndex increments, T-2 chunks stop being wanted (existing objects either get absorbed, fade by the 0.05×radius rule, or ring-despawn — no mass deletion frame), and T+2 of the new tier starts spawning sparsely beyond fog. Absorption removes the object from its hash bucket (swap-remove from the cell array, O(1)), frees the world instance slot after the 0.15 s attach animation, and increments the chunk's absorbed-ID set.

fileLayout.responsibility: Canvas element, HUD/title DOM skeleton, loads /src/main.js as module
Vite build config (base path, chunk splitting off, target es2022)
Cloudflare Pages cache/immutable headers for hashed assets
Boot: construct all systems, wire EventBus subscriptions, start loop; owns nothing else
requestAnimationFrame driver with fixed-timestep (1/60) accumulator, update/render callbacks, rolling frame-time stats
Tiny typed EventBus (on/off/emit), the only cross-module channel besides constructor injection
mulberry32 PRNG + 2D integer hash for chunk seeding
Critically-damped spring, easing functions, formatLength(realMeters)→{value,unit}, scratch Vector3 pool
TIERS array (TierConfig[]): bands, names, fog/sky palettes, chunk densities — single source of scale truth
36 Archetype definitions with build() recipes, size ranges, weights, palettes
All feel constants (accel/maxSpeed/turn multipliers, camera ratios, FOV kick, fade times, budgets) — the designer's file
GameState shape + reset(); run seed, absorbed count, elapsed time, phase (title/playing/paused)
Ball sim: input→velocity, rolling-without-slipping quaternion, ground spring, sluggishness, exposes BallState
Uniform-grid hash (insert/remove/queryNearby/rebuild) over WorldObject ids; two instances (small/large)
Overlap resolution: absorb vs bounce decision, volume-growth math, knock-off, emits absorb/bounce/sizeChanged
StuckRecord store, attach-animation queue, stuck pool slot management, crust bake scheduler (3-frame amortized)
ScaleState owner: tierIndex tracking, tierUp celebration orchestration, one-frame world rescale
Chunk diffing, deterministic generation, spawn/despawn queues with per-frame budgets, absorbed-ID memory
WebGLRenderer setup, resize, pixelRatio cap, dynamic-resolution governor
InstancedPool class: free-list slots, setMatrix/setColor, zero-scale kill, fade animator, rewriteAll for rescale
Builds merged primitive BufferGeometries with vertex colors from Archetype.build recipes at boot
Icosphere core with noise-displacement vertex shader, absorb-color blending, lumpiness uniform
Spring-damped follow camera, look-ahead, lazy yaw, FOV kick, micro-shake
Ground plane with radius-scaled procedural grid shader, sky gradient dome, fog, lights, blob shadow quad
Absorb pop quads, tierUp sparkle ring, speed lines — all instanced, pooled, asset-free
Keyboard (WASD/arrows) + optional mouse yaw + touch joystick → normalized Intent {x,y,boost}
DOM HUD: size odometer with unit rollover, absorbed counter, tier name, progress bar to next tier; subscribes to events only
Title screen, pause, restart overlay; emits gameStart/restart
WebAudio-synthesized blips (rising-pitch absorb combo), clonk, roll loop, tierUp arpeggio — zero audio assets

risks: Rescale-frame spike: rewriting ~3k+ instance matrices plus two hash rebuilds in one frame could exceed 4 ms on weak laptops. Mitigation: measure early with a stress scene; keep object pools SoA Float32Array-backed so the rewrite is a tight loop; rescale trigger has hysteresis (fires at simRadius 20, lands at 4) so it happens at most once every ~90 s; if still hot, halve trigger range so fewer objects are live at trigger time. Hard rule: rescale and crust-bake may never run the same frame (scheduler enforces).

Crust bake hitch: mergeGeometries of 150×220-vert objects on the main thread is ~3–4 ms; on slow machines it may push a frame over budget. Mitigation already designed (3-frame amortization, pre-allocated target buffers); fallback: shrink batch to 60 and bake more often; last resort: skip crust entirely and hard-delete sub-pixel stuck objects (visual cost is near zero, the lumpiness uniform preserves the 'history' feel).

Scale-invariant speed can make growth feel flat — if everything is proportional, the player may stop noticing they're growing. This is a FEEL risk, the lens's top concern. Counters built in (camera spring lag, FOV kicks, fog palette shifts, odometer unit rollovers, next-tier scenery looming) but these need real tuning passes; budget 20–30% of project time for tuning /src/config/tuning.js with a live-reload debug panel.

Stuck-object socket drift: stuck instance matrices are written once at attach in ball-local space, so as the ball grows the embed depth (0.92×radius-at-attach) becomes relatively shallower and objects can appear to float above a much larger ball. Mitigation: retire-to-crust threshold (relative size < 0.04) fires long before drift is visible; if QA still sees it, add a cheap per-pool uniform that re-projects dirLocal×currentRadius×0.92 in the vertex shader.

Sphere-only collision proxies make long/flat objects (bench, bus, bridge span) absorbable/bouncy at odd distances. Mitigation: per-archetype collisionScale fudge factor in the catalog; upgrade hero archetypes to 2–3 sphere clusters only if playtests flag it.

Per-frame instanceMatrix uploads: fades + spawns touch up to ~56 matrices/frame across many InstancedMeshes, each marking its whole buffer needsUpdate. With 36+ pools this is real bandwidth on integrated GPUs. Mitigation: budgets already cap writes; group fade-active slots so most pools are untouched most frames; r177 supports partial buffer updates (updateRanges) — use them in InstancedPool from day one.

Deterministic chunk regeneration vs absorbed-object memory: the per-chunk absorbed-ID Sets grow unboundedly on long runs. Mitigation: forget records for tiers ≥2 below current (those objects are sub-pixel and despawned anyway); cap total memory at ~50k ids.

Fog as the universal pop-hider assumes a fog-friendly art direction; a clear-sky look would expose spawn edges. Accept the constraint: tier palettes are designed around atmospheric haze (it also sells scale, like real distance haze on buildings).

Browser variance: Safari WebGL2 perf and 120 Hz ProMotion laptops (rAF at 120) need the fixed-timestep accumulator and dynamic-resolution governor tested on real hardware, not just Chrome on a dev machine.

lens: SIMPLICITY-FIRST: smallest robust architecture that still nails seamless scale + 60fps. One renderer trick (instancing), one physics trick (sphere + spatial hash), one precision trick (origin rebase), zero discrete scale switches.
summary: A single continuous world with NO discrete scale switching is the simplest design that satisfies the seamless-tier requirement: the ball radius is just a number that grows from 0.08 m to 150+ m, the camera distance/near/far/fog are smooth functions of that number, and "tiers" are purely a content-gating concept (which object families spawn, which despawn) — so there is literally nothing that can pop or hitch at a threshold. Physics is custom arcade physics (~300 lines): a kinematic sphere driven in camera space, with overlap tests against static objects via one uniform spatial hash per tier; no physics library (rapier wasm is ~2 MB and solves problems this game does not have). Rendering is one InstancedMesh per object type (procedurally merged primitive geometry with vertex colors), giving ~20-35 draw calls total regardless of object count; world objects, spawn/despawn animations, and stuck-to-ball objects all live in the same instance pools, just in different states. Stuck objects are pure visuals (the collision sphere stays a sphere), positioned each frame from a stored ball-local offset, and culled when buried (attachRadius < 0.55 × currentRadius), capping stuck render cost at 250 instances forever. Spawning is deterministic per-tier grid-cell hashing in an annulus around the ball, with a consumed-ID set so eaten objects never respawn; far spawns appear beyond the fog so streaming is invisible. Float precision is handled by a floating-origin rebase (shift everything by −ballPos when |ballPos| > 2 km), done in one frame between update and render so it is visually identical. The codebase is ~16 small ES modules in plain JS + JSDoc, decoupled through a 30-line event bus and a single config.js holding every tunable, so 4-5 developers can work in parallel (catalog, world/spawning, physics, rendering, HUD) against the interfaces below. Target budgets: ≤ 35 draw calls, ≤ 450 k worst-case triangles, ≤ 4 ms update + ≤ 8 ms render on a mid laptop, ~150 kB gzipped JS total.
scaleSystem:
CORE IDEA — tiers gate CONTENT, never COORDINATES. The world stays in meters forever; the ball radius r grows continuously 0.08 m → 150+ m; camera, fog, near/far are continuous functions of r. Because no coordinate transform, no camera jump, and no batch swap ever happens at a threshold, seamlessness is structural, not something to be tuned.

TIER TABLE (ball RADIUS in meters; thresholds are entry points, with ±10% hysteresis to prevent thrash):
- T0 "desk":   r 0.08 – 0.30   | objects 0.01–0.25 m  (thumbtack, coin, eraser, die, pencil, candy)
- T1 "yard":   r 0.30 – 1.5    | objects 0.25–1.2 m   (cone, mailbox, dog, bench, bicycle, trash can)
- T2 "street": r 1.5  – 7.0    | objects 1.2–6 m      (car, tree, kiosk, van, streetlight, bus stop)
- T3 "block":  r 7.0  – 35     | objects 6–28 m       (house, truck, billboard, water tower, big tree)
- T4 "city":   r 35   – 150+   | objects 28–120 m     (building, ferris wheel, ship, stadium, crane)
tierOf(r) = largest t with TIERS[t].minR <= r, applied with hysteresis: only change current tier when r crosses minR*1.1 upward or minR*0.9 downward.

WHAT A TIER CONTROLS (and the ONLY things it controls):
1. Active spawn set = tiers {t-1, t, t+1} (clamped). Tier t+1 cells start streaming in the moment t is entered — but the spawn annulus outer radius sits beyond the fog wall, so new big objects materialize invisibly and are simply "discovered" by rolling toward them.
2. Despawn set = tiers <= t-2: their world instances stop spawning and existing ones play a 0.8 s shrink-into-ground animation (per-instance scale in the instance matrix — no per-instance alpha needed, which keeps instancing trivial), throttled to ≤ 60 animation starts per frame, farthest-from-ball first. By the time the player is in T2, every T0 instance slot has been freed.
3. Absorbability is NOT tier-based at all — it is the continuous rule objSize < 1.4·r (i.e. object smaller than ~0.7× ball diameter). Objects between 1.4·r and 4·r are solid obstacles (push-out collision); objects > 4·r are scenery you bonk off gently.

CONTINUOUS CAMERA / DEPTH (all functions of r, exp-damped with halflife 0.25 s):
- camDist = 7·r, camHeight = 3.2·r, lookAt = ballPos + 1.0·r up.
- near = max(0.02, 0.06·r); far = 600·r clamped to [80, 60000]. Because near scales with r, depth precision is constant across 3+ orders of magnitude — no logarithmicDepthBuffer needed (kept as a one-line fallback flag in config).
- fog = THREE.Fog(skyColor, 60·r, 360·r). Fog start/end scale with r, so the visible "bubble" always contains the same ~3 tiers of content and hides all streaming.

GROWTH MATH (player.js): on absorb, newR = cbrt(r³ + GROWTH_K · (objSize/2)³) with GROWTH_K = 0.55 (tuned in config). r is additionally slewed visually at max 1.5·r per second so a big meal swells smoothly instead of stepping.

TIER TRANSITION ALGORITHM (runs once per frame in world.js):
```
t = tierWithHysteresis(ball.r)
if t != currentTier:
    currentTier = t
    emit('tierchange', {tier:t, name:TIERS[t].name})   // HUD flourish only
for each tier k in 0..4:
    if k in [t-1, t, t+1]: tierState[k] = ACTIVE       // streams cells
    elif k == t-2:         tierState[k] = DRAINING     // no new spawns, shrink-out existing
    else:                  tierState[k] = DORMANT      // all slots freed
```
Note the emit is cosmetic; gameplay/camera/rendering never branch on the event.

FLOAT PRECISION — FLOATING-ORIGIN REBASE (game.js, ~30 lines):
World coordinates are float64 in JS but float32 on GPU and in instance matrices. At 2 km from origin float32 still has ~0.25 mm steps (fine for 1 cm thumbtacks), so:
```
if length(ball.pos.xz) > 2000:
    offset = -round(ball.pos.xz)          // integer meters, avoids sub-mm churn
    shift ball.pos, camera.pos, every WORLD/ANIM instance position by offset
    rewrite affected instance matrices (≤ ~2000 composeMatrix calls, <0.5 ms)
    shift world.originOffset by -offset    // spawn cells are keyed in UNSHIFTED
                                           // "global" coords: globalPos = localPos - originOffset
    rebuild spatial hashes (cheap: re-insert ≤2000 entries)
```
Runs between update() and render() in the same frame → pixel-identical output, zero hitch. Spawn-cell keys and the consumed-ID set always use global coordinates so determinism survives rebases. Vertical axis never drifts (flat ground at y=0).

WHY THIS IS SEAMLESS, SUMMARIZED: (1) nothing ever teleports or rescales on screen; (2) camera/fog/near/far are smooth functions of r; (3) tier changes only toggle spawn eligibility, and all appearance/disappearance is fog-hidden or scale-animated; (4) hysteresis prevents boundary flicker; (5) rebase is integer-snapped and intra-frame.
physics:
DECISION: custom arcade physics. NO physics library.

Justification for THIS game: (a) The only dynamic body is the katamari — a sphere on a flat plane. Every other object is static scenery until absorbed (becomes a visual) or acts as an obstacle (a positional push-out). No stacks, no joints, no resting contacts, no object-vs-object dynamics — i.e. none of the problems rapier3d (~1.7 MB wasm + glue, async init, per-body handles for thousands of streamed statics) or cannon-es (~150 kB, still requires mirroring pooled/streamed objects into its broadphase) actually solve. (b) Katamari "feel" is arcade, not physical: we need direct authorship of acceleration, turn rate, and bonk response; a real solver fights that tuning. (c) Bundle: ~300 lines, 0 dependency bytes; app stays ~150 kB gzipped (three.js dominates). (d) Debuggability in a one-day build: a sphere-overlap bug is a console.log; a wasm solver bug is an afternoon.

MODEL (physics.js + player.js; variable dt clamped to 33 ms, semi-implicit Euler):
- State: pos (y = r always — ground is the analytic plane y=0, the single biggest simplification this design buys), horizontal vel, visual roll quat, radius r.
- Input → accel in camera-yaw space: ACCEL = 9·r m/s² (scaling with r keeps screen-relative feel constant across tiers). MAX_SPEED = 3.5·r. Friction: vel *= exp(-1.8·dt).
- Visual rolling: axis = up × vel̂, angle = |vel|·dt / r; quat = axisAngle(axis, angle) ∘ quat. Cosmetic only, never solved.
- Vs bigger objects (objSize ≥ 1.4·r): object approximated as a sphere of radius objSize/2; on overlap, push ball out along the contact normal and remove the normal velocity component (slide). If impact speed > 0.7·MAX_SPEED → emit('bonk') and eject 1–3 newest stuck objects (state EJECT: small ballistic arc, then become WORLD again or fade) — classic Katamari feedback, purely visual.
- Vs smaller objects (objSize < 1.4·r): absorb on overlap.

SPATIAL HASH (spatialHash.js): one uniform grid PER TIER (5 instances) because object sizes span 4 orders of magnitude. cellSize[t] = 4 × TIERS[t].maxObjSize → [1, 5, 24, 112, 480] m. Map<string,'ix,iz' → int[] slotIds>. Objects are static: insert once at spawn, remove at absorb/despawn; zero per-frame maintenance.

PER-FRAME COLLISION PSEUDOCODE:
```
function queryCircle(hash, cx, cz, radius, out):
    for ix in floor((cx-radius)/cell)..floor((cx+radius)/cell):
      for iz in floor((cz-radius)/cell)..floor((cz+radius)/cell):
        bucket = map.get(ix+','+iz); if bucket: out.push(...bucket)

frame:
    out.length = 0                                   // reused array, no GC
    for t in activeTiers(curTier-1..curTier+1):
        queryCircle(hash[t], ball.x, ball.z, ball.r + cell[t], out)
    for slot in out:                                 // typically 5–40 candidates
        o = instances[slot]; if o.state != WORLD: continue
        rr = ball.r + o.size*0.5
        if distSq(ball.pos, o.pos) > rr*rr: continue
        if o.size < 1.4*ball.r: absorb(slot)
        else: pushOut(ball, o)
```
Cost: tens of distance checks per frame, no allocations, no rebuilds.
rendering:
STRATEGY: one InstancedMesh per object TYPE; everything an object can be (placed in world, spawning-in, despawning-out, stuck to ball, flying off after a bonk) is just a state of an instance slot in that type's pool. One code path, one upload path, flat draw-call count.

DRAW-CALL BUDGET (hard cap 40, typical ~28):
- 6 object types/tier × 3 active tiers = ≤ 18 InstancedMesh draws (DORMANT tiers' meshes have count=0 → skipped by three.js).
- Ball (1 sphere, vertex-colored two-tone Katamari pattern), ground (1 plane), blob shadow under ball (1 disc), sky (background color — free), optional 1 instanced "confetti" burst pool for absorb feedback. HUD is DOM, zero GL cost.

INSTANCE POOLS (render/instances.js, class InstancedPool): capacity 512 per type, allocated once at boot (30 types × 512 matrices ≈ 1 MB GPU — trivial). Slot states: FREE / WORLD / SPAWNING / DESPAWNING / STUCK / EJECT. Hidden slots get a zero-scale matrix (cheaper and simpler than re-packing). Per frame, only dirty slots recompose matrices; instanceMatrix.needsUpdate set once per pool that changed. STUCK and animating slots are dirty every frame but bounded: ≤ 250 stuck + ≤ ~120 animating → ≤ 400 Matrix4.compose calls/frame ≈ 0.2 ms.

GEOMETRY/MATERIAL (catalog.js): each type is ONE BufferGeometry built at boot by merging transformed primitives (Box/Cylinder/Cone/Sphere/Torus) via BufferGeometryUtils.mergeGeometries, with COLORS BAKED AS VERTEX COLORS — so all 30 types share a single MeshLambertMaterial({vertexColors:true}). Budget ≤ 350 triangles per type (a car = 2 boxes + 4 cylinders ≈ 180 tris). Worst-case on-screen: ~1,500 world instances × ~250 avg tris + ball/ground ≈ 400–450 k tris — comfortably 60 fps with Lambert + 1 directional + 1 hemisphere light, NO shadow maps (the blob disc is the shadow). pixelRatio capped at 1.5, antialias:true.

STUCK-OBJECT HANDLING (stuck.js):
- On absorb: keep the same instance slot, switch state to STUCK, store ball-local attachment:
```
absorb(slot):
    o = instances[slot]
    local = invQuat(ball.quat) * (o.pos - ball.pos)        // ball-space offset
    local = normalize(local) * (ball.r + o.size*0.18)      // embed ~1/3 into surface
    o.localPos = local; o.localQuat = invQuat(ball.quat) * o.worldQuat
    o.attachR = ball.r; stuckList.push(slot)
    hash[o.tier].remove(slot); consumed.add(o.globalId)
    player.grow(o.size); emit('absorb', {...})
every frame, for slot in stuckList:                         // ≤ 250
    world = ball.pos + ball.quat * o.localPos
    matrix = compose(world, ball.quat * o.localQuat, o.size)
```
- BURIAL CULL (the render-cost limiter): localPos length is fixed at stick time, so as r grows the object sinks under newer layers. When o.attachR < 0.55 · ball.r → it is invisible inside the ball: free the slot (HUD absorbed count unaffected). Additionally hard-cap stuckList at 250, freeing most-buried first. Result: stuck render cost is O(250) forever, from thumbtacks to skyscrapers, with NO merge/bake pipeline — burial culling IS the bake, and it is 5 lines. The ball always looks lumpy because the newest ~250 absorptions are by definition the visible surface layer.

CULLING & STREAMING: per-InstancedMesh frustum culling stays ON (whole-pool bounding sphere); per-instance culling is intentionally NOT done — the fog bubble + spawn annulus already bound live instance counts, and zero-scale matrices make hidden slots near-free. Spawn/despawn animate scale 0→1 / 1→0 over 0.4 s / 0.8 s (ease-out-back pop-in, anyway hidden behind fog).

FRAME BUDGET on a typical laptop (M1 / Iris Xe class): input+physics+streaming ≤ 2 ms, matrix updates ≤ 1 ms, three.js render ≤ 8 ms, headroom ≥ 5 ms.
spawning:
OBJECT CATALOG: 30 types = 6 per tier. Each entry (catalog.js) declares: id, tier, nominal sizeM, sizeJitter ±25%, weight (spawn frequency), palette (3–4 hex colors), and build() returning a merged vertex-colored BufferGeometry. Examples — T0: thumbtack 0.02 m (cylinder+cone), coin 0.025, die 0.02, eraser 0.05, candy 0.04, pencil 0.18; T1: cone 0.4, trash can 0.9, dog 0.7 (boxes+cylinders), mailbox 1.1, bench 1.2, bicycle 1.1; T2: kiosk 3, streetlight 4, car 4.2 (2 boxes + 4 cylinder wheels), tree 5 (cone+cylinder), van 5.5, bus stop 4; T3: house 9 (box+prism roof), truck 12, billboard 14, big tree 16, water tower 20, small building 26; T4: water tank 40, building 60–120 (stacked boxes), crane 70, ferris wheel 80 (torus+cylinders), ship 90, stadium 100.

DETERMINISTIC CELL STREAMING (world.js): each tier has a spawn grid with spawnCell[t] = 16 × 4^t m → [16, 64, 256, 1024, 4096]. For cell (ix, iz, t), mulberry32(hash3(ix,iz,t) ^ RUN_SEED) generates count and, per object: weighted type, position uniform in cell, yaw, size jitter. Same cell → same objects, always; the only persistent world state is the consumed set.

DENSITY BUDGET: objectsPerCell tuned so each ACTIVE tier carries ~350–500 live instances in its annulus → ≤ ~1,500 live world instances total across 3 active tiers (fits 6 pools × 512 with slack; pool-full overflow objects are silently skipped — invisible at these densities).

ANNULUS STREAMING (per active tier; re-evaluated when ball moves ≥ spawnCell[t]/4 or tier state changes):
```
outer[t] = max(380*ball.r, 2*spawnCell[t])      // just past fog end (360r)
for each cell of tier t within outer[t] and not realized: realize(cell)
for each realized cell beyond 1.25*outer[t]: derealize(cell)   // hysteresis ring
realize(cell):  for each generated o:
    if consumed.has(o.globalId): skip
    slot = pool[o.type].alloc(); state = SPAWNING(0.4s); hash[t].insert(slot)
derealize(cell): free slots + hash.remove        // instant — beyond fog, invisible
```
globalId = `${t}:${ix}:${iz}:${k}` in ORIGIN-UNSHIFTED global coordinates (cell indices computed as localPos - originOffset, so determinism survives floating-origin rebases). Stored in Set<string>; a long session ≈ 3–5 k absorbed ids → negligible memory.

TIER DRAIN (tier t-2 → DRAINING): realized cells stop being added; existing instances start DESPAWNING (0.8 s shrink-into-ground, farthest-from-ball first, ≤ 60 starts/frame). By construction this happens while those objects subtend < ~2 px on screen, so it reads as nothing at all.

GROUND: one 8×8-segment plane + generated 512² CanvasTexture (soft two-tone checker); plane follows the ball snapped to texture-repeat multiples (no visible swimming), repeat scale stepped at tier boundaries while the previous scale crossfades via a second plane during 1 s — OR, simpler accepted default: fixed 4 m checker that naturally becomes fine-grain noise at distance under fog. Start with the simple default; the crossfade plane is a stretch goal.

fileLayout.responsibility: Entry HTML: canvas mount point, HUD DOM skeleton (size readout, counter, progress), title/restart overlay markup, loads src/main.js as module.
Boot only: construct renderer/scene/camera, instantiate all modules in dependency order, wire the event bus, start the RAF loop. No game logic (~60 lines).

Single source of ALL tunables: TIERS array, physics constants (ACCEL_K, MAX_SPEED_K, FRICTION), GROWTH_K, camera multipliers, fog multipliers, pool capacities, stuck cap (250), rebase distance (2000 m), RUN_SEED.
Minimal pub/sub bus: on(name, fn), off, emit(name, payload). ~30 lines; the only coupling channel between game core and HUD/audio-feedback.
Seeded RNG (mulberry32), hash3(ix,iz,t), damp(current,target,halflife,dt), clamp/lerp, shared scratch Vector3/Quaternion/Matrix4 temps to keep the game allocation-free per frame.
Orchestrator + state machine (TITLE/PLAYING/PAUSED): fixed update order (input → player → physics → stuck → world → camera → pools), dt clamping, floating-origin rebase, restart/reset.
Keyboard (WASD/arrows) + optional mouse-steer + touch joystick → normalized {x, z} move vector in camera-yaw space; no game knowledge.
Katamari state (pos, vel, quat, radius + visual slewed radius): input integration, movement/friction, visual roll quat, grow(sizeM) volume math, emits 'grow'.
Per-frame collision pass: spatial-hash queries around ball, absorb-vs-pushOut dispatch, bonk detection + stuck ejection trigger. Pure functions over instance records.
SpatialHash class (uniform grid, Map<'ix,iz', slotId[]>): insert/remove/queryCircle with reused output array; instantiated once per tier.
Streaming + tiers: tierWithHysteresis, ACTIVE/DRAINING/DORMANT tier states, deterministic cell realize/derealize in annulus, consumed-ID set, spawn/despawn animation scheduling, emits 'tierchange'.
Stuck-object manager: attach math (ball-local offset/quat), per-frame stuck matrix recompute, burial culling (attachR < 0.55·r), 250-cap eviction, bonk ejection.
The 30 ObjectType definitions (id, tier, sizeM, jitter, weight, palette) + buildGeometry() composite-primitive builders with baked vertex colors; exports CATALOG and byTier index.
Three.js setup: WebGLRenderer (pixelRatio ≤ 1.5), scene, hemisphere + directional light (no shadow maps), fog, ground plane + CanvasTexture, ball mesh + blob shadow, resize handling.
Follow camera as pure function of ball radius/position/heading: distance/height/lookAt multipliers, exp damping, continuous near/far/fog updates.
InstancedPool class: one InstancedMesh per type, slot alloc/free, state enum, dirty-slot matrix recompose, scale animations (SPAWNING/DESPAWNING/EJECT), zero-scale hiding, needsUpdate flagging.
DOM-only HUD: subscribes to bus ('grow','absorb','tierchange','state'); size formatter (mm/cm/m/km), absorbed counter, tier-progress bar, title screen + restart button wiring.

risks: Growth pacing / game-feel tuning is the real schedule risk, not tech: GROWTH_K, ACCEL_K and per-cell densities interact; mitigate by putting every constant in config.js and adding a dev-only query param (?r=5) to start at any radius for instant tier testing.

Instance-matrix upload spikes: if a tier drain or rebase dirties a whole 512-slot pool in one frame, full-buffer uploads for several pools could cost 1-2 ms; mitigated by the 60-starts/frame drain throttle and integer-snapped rebase, but profile early on Intel iGPUs.

Z-fighting / depth precision at extreme r: the near-plane-scales-with-r scheme must be verified at r=0.08 (near 0.02) and r=150 (near 9); blob shadow and ground need polygonOffset or small y-epsilons that also scale with r.

Floating-origin rebase is rarely exercised in normal play and therefore rots: force-test it with a dev key that teleports the ball to 1990 m, and assert spawn determinism (same globalIds) across a rebase in a unit check.

Spawn density vs pool capacity: if a tier's annulus needs more than 512 instances of one popular type, objects silently skip and the world looks sparse in patches; needs a one-line console.warn counter during tuning.

Stuck-object visual quality: burial culling at attachR<0.55r plus the 250 cap may make the ball look 'molting' right after a tier jump if many same-age objects cull together; mitigate by staggering culls over ~1 s.

Variable-dt arcade physics can tunnel through small objects at high speed (ball moves > objSize per frame); mitigate with a swept check (segment-vs-sphere) only for candidates already returned by the hash — cheap, ~10 lines.
Consumed-set determinism depends on cell-generation order never changing; lock the RNG draw order in catalog/world code review (any reorder of draws changes every world).
